speaker: interviewee
text: Those two are very much alike.

speaker: interviewer
text: Steve, what about Brunne Schultz?

speaker: interviewee
text: Who? This is my mother, Romana Halperin. It's a painting by a famous Polish painter, Ignacy Witkiewicz, sometimes known as Witkacy. It was done in 1928. He has painted my mother many, many times.

speaker: interviewer
text: Why don't you tell me why your mother? Why he painted her?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother was very much interested in artists and art. And she was studying-- she was studying in a theater, actually. She was the one that made me very interested in theater. Anyhow, the Witkiewicz painter liked her, I guess, as a model. And he painted her many times. He even painted my picture. But it was lost during the war.

speaker: interviewer
text: These-- these paintings, are there originals left anywhere?

speaker: interviewee
text: The original is hanging on my wall in the room right here. There's another one hanging in the house of my daughter, in Woodland Hills. There are two, which are hanging at my best friend's in New York. I told him he can have them right now. And that's about it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And is this book-- are there any other paintings of your mother in this book?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, this is the only painting of my mother in this book. The whole book is the painter's Witkiewicz paintings. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] This is a counterfeit birth certificate for myself, which states that my name is Stefan Kasimien Halicki, a very Polish name, born in Lida, L-I-D-A, Poland, which at that time was under Russian occupation. And my mother obtained this for me before I left the ghetto so that I could pass outside, as-- well, as a Pole and not as a Jew. I made it look old on purpose in order so that the Germans wouldn't be suspicious about how new it was.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you-- was this from-- which ghetto did you receive this paper.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Warsaw. When I left the Warsaw ghetto, my mother gave me this before I left. She has obtained this by whatever means, I don't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year did you-- did you get this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I left the ghetto in July of 1942. [PAUSES FOR 10 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: [? Bet she was pretty ?]

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a wedding picture of myself and my wife, Bozenna Howard, known also as Bibi. This happened on August 6th, 1960. My name at that time was Stephen Jay Howard.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I arrived in the United States, I was still under the impression-- well, impression is the wrong word-- of anti-Semitism, which existed in Poland and Germany. And I guess I was afraid that I would encounter the same in the United States. And I had enough of this for six years. And somebody says, why don't you change your name? So I opened a telephone book. And there was Howard. And I became a Howard. Where was-- where was your wedding? Where did you--

speaker: interviewee
text: The wedding was here in Los Angeles [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at the University of Southern California. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] This is a picture taken at a wedding of my wife's niece, Allegra Mancuso in 1992. On the left is my wife, Bibi Howard. Next to her is her sister, Fredericka Mancuso. To the right, in the middle, is her mother, Fredericka Blevicz. Next to her is another niece, Julia Valensky, and her mother Alexandra Valensky on the right-hand side, my wife's sister.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where was the photograph taken?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was taken at the--

speaker: interviewer
text: In what city?

speaker: interviewee
text: Wedding in Los Angeles, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: This is a picture of my stepson, Richard Sawicki, S-A-W-I-C-K-I, who is now 38. And he certainly is exactly how he looks like because the picture was taken yesterday. This is my wife's son from her previous marriage.

speaker: interviewer
text: And so meaning yesterday, could you give the date then?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was August 6th, 1995. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And this is a picture of my daughter, Jackie, Jacqueline Hebert, and her husband-- [BACKGROUND NOISE] her husband, Robert Hebert. Actually, it's spelled H-E-B-E-R-T. And it's 'eh-bare,' because it's a French name. But here in the United States, nobody says this. They all say 'hee-bert.' It was done in 1990 in Los Angeles, California.

speaker: interviewer
text: And that's the last photograph.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, no, we have-- [INTERPOSING VOICES] this is the last photograph of my family, yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right. That-- that we'll and I want to-- I think that's it. So I want to thank you again very much.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mm-hm. You're welcome.
